question: What is the manufacturing cluster readiness - can Chinese OEM factories actually build global brands?

findings.claim: The 'Smiling Curve' theory remains highly relevant: Chinese manufacturing sits at the low-value middle of the value chain, with R&D and brand/marketing at the high-value ends
findings.data: Smiling Curve (proposed by Acer founder Stan Shih, 1992): In the value chain, added value is concentrated at two ends - design/R&D (left) and marketing/sales (right), while manufacturing in the middle has lowest added value. Since joining WTO, China positioned at the curve's bottom via cheap production factors for OEM work. Most profits flow to developed countries. Recommended strategies to move up: (1) product upgrading/market expansion for economies of scale; (2) vertical integration; (3) shortening sales channels for higher added value.

findings.claim: CE and FDA certifications have shifted from competitive differentiators to mandatory entry requirements for Chinese OEM exporters, creating a significant barrier
findings.data: Quality control and international safety certifications (UL, CE) are now 'non-negotiable entry barriers, not just competitive advantages' for the $1.2T global consumer electronics market by 2026. North American and European buyers account for 65%+ of total international inquiries and require certified products. Chinese manufacturers face language barriers, lack of information, and are often too small for dedicated certification teams. Tariffs on electronics components including li-ion batteries rose in 2025 (25% tariff on lithium battery parts newly introduced).

findings.claim: Made in China 2025 broadly succeeded at the sector level according to USCC assessment, but individual company brand-building success is far more uneven
findings.data: U.S.-China Economic and Security Review Commission (Nov 2025) evaluated MIC2025 across key technology sectors: 'China broadly succeeded in reaching its ambitious targets' in terms of global market share and top global companies. However, at the individual company level, no widely cited success rate exists for OEM-to-brand transitions. A thriving consulting industry has emerged specifically to help SMEs navigate the transition, suggesting both demand and difficulty. One firm claims serving 170+ enterprises, helping 30+ become segment leaders - implying roughly 18% 'leadership' success rate among clients.

findings.claim: By 2025, 70%+ of large Chinese manufacturing firms have substantially completed digital networking and built smart factories, providing infrastructure for brand-quality production
findings.data: By 2025, more than 70% of large manufacturing firms in China have substantially completed digital networking and built demonstrator 'smart factories.' China's Feb 2026 manufacturing PMI rose to 52.1 (highest since Dec 2020), with output growing the most since June 2024 and foreign demand seeing its sharpest rise since September 2020. The government's 'anti-involution' campaign aims to consolidate overcapacity sectors.

findings.claim: China's luggage/bag exports reached 368M tons in 2025 but export value declined 13.5% YoY to $29.9B, indicating deepening price competition and lack of brand premium
findings.data: 2025 full-year Chinese luggage/bag exports: 368M tons (+1.6% YoY in volume) but $29.9B (-13.5% YoY in value). Volume up but value down sharply demonstrates the exact 'smiling curve' problem: manufacturing scale without brand pricing power. Average export price per unit declining significantly.
